exercises.title: A: Multiple Choice (Podcasting text)
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct answer (A, B, C or D) for each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct answer (A, B, C or D) for each gap.
exercises.passage: Before podcasting went mainstream, it was (1) ...... for granted that audio content could only be produced by trained professionals with expensive studio equipment. In other (2) ...... , listeners were simply passive recipients of whatever topics producers chose to broadcast. The concept has certainly (3) ...... on and, for tens of millions of users, independent audio shows now (4) ...... a real appetite for niche, in-depth storytelling. Leading podcast networks (5) ...... that the format encourages deeper engagement than traditional radio because listeners actively choose every episode they consume. Despite this, media watchdogs have (6) ...... several popular shows for spreading health misinformation without editorial oversight. Certain programmes have indeed (7) ...... unverified claims as established medical or scientific fact. However, although these incidents have received wide media (8) ...... , they arguably (9) ...... the extent of misinformation that existed in older broadcast formats. Most listeners have little (10) ...... of how much unverified content circulated freely on conventional radio long before digital alternatives emerged.

exercises.title: B: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
My boss is extremely displeased with me because I arrived late three days in a row. **books**
I ................................................................. because I arrived late three days in a row.
items.answer: am in my boss's bad books
acceptedAnswers: am in my boss's bad books

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is in trouble with boss, so the correct answer is "am in my boss's bad books".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "am in my boss's bad books", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Nobody needs to be told that first impressions at a job interview matter enormously. **saying**
It ................................................................. that first impressions at a job interview matter enormously.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is obvious, so the correct answer is "goes without saying".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "goes without saying", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
She hasn't confirmed anything, but my interpretation of the signs is that the director is planning to leave the company. **lines**
She hasn't confirmed anything, but ................................................................. , I'd say the director is planning to leave the company.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is infer indirectly, so the correct answer is "reading between the lines".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "reading between the lines", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Alex and Daniel haven't spoken to each other since the argument last month. **terms**
Alex and Daniel are no longer ................................................................. since the argument last month.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not speaking, so the correct answer is "on speaking terms".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
It was wrong of you to persuade Marco to keep quiet about the incident. **talked**
You should ................................................................. about the incident.
items.answer: not have talked Marco into keeping quiet
acceptedAnswers: not have talked Marco into keeping quiet

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is shouldn't have persuaded, so the correct answer is "not have talked Marco into keeping quiet".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "not have talked Marco into keeping quiet", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
I'm certain the manager spoke to Claire about the complaints before the meeting. **word**
The manager ................................................................. the complaints before the meeting.
items.answer: must have had a word with Claire about
acceptedAnswers: must have had a word with Claire about

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is certain past deduction, so the correct answer is "must have had a word with Claire about".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "must have had a word with Claire about", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
I don't think Patrick misunderstood what you said, Sophie. **stick**
Patrick can't ................................................................., Sophie.
items.answer: have got the wrong end of the stick
acceptedAnswers: have got the wrong end of the stick

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is misunderstood, so the correct answer is "have got the wrong end of the stick".

wrongOptions.got the wrong end of the stick: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have got the wrong end of the stick", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
There's no need for you to make any promises – I trust you completely. **word**
You ................................................................. – I trust you completely.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no promise needed, so the correct answer is "needn't give me your word".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "needn't give me your word", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "needn't give me your word" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

exercises.title: C: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: Under his inspiring ...... (EDIT) , the university newspaper won several national awards.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from EDIT, so the correct answer is "editorship".

wrongOptions.edit: The root EDIT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "editorship".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of EDIT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: EDIT → editorship: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "editorship" is the word-formation answer from the root EDIT in this context.

items.sentence: Most of what she told the police was pure ...... (HEAR) – none of the neighbours had actually witnessed the event.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is rumour not fact, so the correct answer is "hearsay".

wrongOptions.hear: The root HEAR cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "hearsay".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of HEAR that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: HEAR → hearsay: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "hearsay" is the word-formation answer from the root HEAR in this context.

items.sentence: The closure of the mine will have widespread ...... (IMPLY) for employment across the entire county.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from IMPLY, so the correct answer is "implications".

wrongOptions.imply: The root IMPLY cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "implications".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of IMPLY that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: IMPLY → implications: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "implications" is the word-formation answer from the root IMPLY in this context.

items.sentence: Describing that concert as 'quite enjoyable' is a bit of a/an ...... (STATE), given how extraordinary the performance was.
